step.title: Step 8
step.actionDisplay: DOWN -> SPACE -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 13 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): DOWN, SPACE, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, UP, UP.
You have progressed to a new level!
Current state: step 28, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I have the full picture:
**Red diamond**: 38 pixels forming a diamond/rhombus shape. The diamond has two diagonal lines:
- One from (21,39) to (39,39) - wait, let me trace it.
- Looking at the positions: (21,39), (22,38), (22,40), (23,37), (23,41), ... this forms a diamond outline.
- The center appears to be around (30, 39). Let me check: (30,30) and (30,39)... no.
- Actually, the diamond is formed by two diagonal lines crossing. Let me find the crossing point.
- Line 1: (21,39), (22,38), (23,37), (24,36), (25,35), (26,34), (27,33), (28,32), (29,31), (30,30), (31,31), (32,32), (33,33), (34,34), (35,35), (36,36), (37,37), (38,38), (39,39)
- Line 2: (21,39), (22,40), (23,41), (24,42), (25,43), (26,44), (27,45), (28,46), (29,47), (30,48)... wait, (30,48) is not in the list. Let me check.
- Line 2: (21,39), (22,40), (23,41), (24,42), (25,43), (26,44), (27,45), (28,46), (29,47), (31,47), (32,46), (33,45), (34,44), (35,43), (36,42), (37,41), (38,40), (39,39)
- Wait, (30,48) is missing from line 2. Let me recheck.
- Actually, looking more carefully: (29,47), then (31,47) - there's a gap at row 30. And (30,30) is in line 1.
- The diamond center is at (30, 39) - but (30,39) is not in the list. Hmm.
- Let me reconsider. The diamond might have center at (30, 39). The two diagonals cross at (30, 39).
- Line 1: row+col = constant. (21,39): 21+39=60. (30,30): 30+30=60. (39,39): 39+39=78. No, that's not right.
- Line 1: row-col = constant. (21,39): 21-39=-18. (22,38): 22-38=-16. No.
- Let me look at it differently. The diamond has 4 sides, each going diagonally.
- Top vertex: (21, 39)
- Going down-left: (22,38), (23,37), (24,36), (25,35), (26,34), (27,33), (28,32), (29,31), (30,30)
- Going down-right: (22,40), (23,41), (24,42), (25,43), (26,44), (27,45), (28,46), (29,47)
- Bottom vertex: (39, 39)
- Going up-left from bottom: (38,38), (37,37), (36,36), (35,35), (34,34), (33,33), (32,32), (31,31), (30,30)
- Going up-right from bottom: (38,40), (37,41), (36,42), (35,43), (34,44), (33,45), (32,46), (31,47), (30,48)... but (30,48) is not in the list!
- Hmm, (30,48) is not in the red pixels. But (30,30) is. So the diamond is not symmetric?
- Actually wait, let me recount. The red pixels include (3,21), (9,27), (12,12) which are NOT part of the diamond. These are the centers of the red-centered gray squares!
- Gray squares with red centers: (2,20) → center (3,21), (8,26) → center (9,27), (11,11) → center (12,12). Yes!
- So the diamond has 38 - 3 = 35 pixels. But a diamond outline should have an even number... Let me recount.
- Actually, 38 red pixels total. 3 are gray square centers. So 35 are diamond pixels.
- A diamond outline: top vertex + 2 sides going down + bottom vertex + 2 sides going up.
- From (21,39) to (30,30): 10 pixels (including both endpoints)
- From (21,39) to (29,47): 9 pixels (including both endpoints)
- From (30,30) to (39,39): 10 pixels (including both endpoints)
- From (39,39) to (29,47): 11 pixels (including both endpoints)
- Total: 10 + 9 + 10 + 11 - 4 (shared vertices) = 36. Hmm, that's 36, not 35.
- Let me just count the actual diamond pixels from the list, excluding (3,21), (9,27), (12,12):
  (21,39), (22,38), (22,40), (23,37), (23,41), (24,36), (24,42), (25,35), (25,43), (26,34), (26,44), (27,33), (27,45), (28,32), (28,46), (29,31), (29,47), (30,30), (31,31), (31,47), (32,32), (32,46), (33,33), (33,45), (34,34), (34,44), (35,35), (35,43), (36,36), (36,42), (37,37), (37,41), (38,38), (38,40), (39,39)
  That's 36 pixels. But we have 38 - 3 = 35. Let me recount the original list...
  Actually, I might be miscounting. Let me just count: the list from (21,39) to (39,39) has 36 entries. Plus (3,21), (9,27), (12,12) = 39. But we said 38 total. Let me recount from the output.
Looking at the output again: 38 red pixels listed. The first 3 are (3,21), (9,27), (12,12) which are gray square centers. The remaining 35 are diamond pixels.
Let me count the diamond pixels from (21,39) to (39,39):
(21,39), (22,38), (22,40), (23,37), (23,41), (24,36), (24,42), (25,35), (25,43), (26,34), (26,44), (27,33), (27,45), (28,32), (28,46), (29,31), (29,47), (30,30), (31,31), (31,47), (32,32), (32,46), (33,33), (33,45), (34,34), (34,44), (35,35), (35,43), (36,36), (36,42), (37,37), (37,41), (38,38), (38,40), (39,39)
That's 35 pixels. OK so the diamond is 35 pixels.
The diamond center is where the two diagonals cross. Looking at the pattern:
- Left diagonal: (21,39), (22,38), ..., (30,30), (31,31), ..., (39,39)
- Right diagonal: (21,39), (22,40), ..., (29,47), (31,47), ..., (39,39)
Wait, (30,48) is missing from the right diagonal. And (30,30) is on the left diagonal.
The crossing point should be at (30, 39) - but (30,39) is not in the list. So the diamond doesn't have a center pixel.
Actually, looking more carefully:
- Left side: (21,39) → (30,30) → (39,39) - this is a V shape
- Right side: (21,39) → (29,47) → (31,47) → (39,39) - this is also a V shape but with a gap at (30,48)
Hmm, this doesn't look like a standard diamond. Let me reconsider.
Actually, I think the diamond is formed by two diagonal lines that cross:
- Line 1: (21,39), (22,38), (23,37), (24,36), (25,35), (26,34), (27,33), (28,32), (29,31), (30,30), (31,31), (32,32), (33,33), (34,34), (35,35), (36,36), (37,37), (38,38), (39,39)
  This is a diagonal line where row+col = 60 for the first half and row-col = 1 for the second half. Wait no.
  (21,39): 21+39=60, (22,38): 22+38=60, ..., (30,30): 30+30=60, (31,31): 31+31=62. So the first part has row+col=60, then it changes.
Hmm, actually this is two lines crossing at (30, 30):
- Line A: row-col = -18 for (21,39), -16 for (22,38)... no.
- Let me try: for the first half, col = 60 - row. For the second half, col = row.
  (21,39): 60-21=39 ✓, (30,30): 60-30=30 ✓, (31,31): 31=31 ✓, (39,39): 39=39 ✓
  So this is a V-shape with vertex at (30, 30).
- Line B: (21,39), (22,40), (23,41), (24,42), (25,43), (26,44), (27,45), (28,46), (29,47), then (31,47), (32,46), (33,45), (34,44), (35,43), (36,42), (37,41), (38,40), (39,39)
  First half: col = row + 18. (21,39): 21+18=39 ✓, (29,47): 29+18=47 ✓
  Second half: col = 86 - row. (31,47): 86-31=55 ≠ 47. Hmm.
  Actually: (31,47), (32,46), (33,45), (34,44), (35,43), (36,42), (37,41), (38,40), (39,39)
  col = 86 - row? 86-31=55 ≠ 47. No.
  col = row + 16? 31+16=47 ✓, 32+16=48 ≠ 46. No.
  Actually: 31+47=78, 32+46=78, 33+45=78, ..., 39+39=78. So row+col=78 for the second half.
  And for the first half: 21+39=60, 22+40=62, ..., 29+47=76. So row+col goes from 60 to 76.
OK so the diamond is formed by:
- Left V: vertex at (30, 30), going up to (21, 39) and down to (39, 39)
- Right V: vertex at (30, 48) [missing], going up to (21, 39) and down to (39, 39)
But (30, 48) is missing! So the right V has a gap at the vertex.
The diamond center is at (30, 39) - the midpoint between (30, 30) and (30, 48).
Wait, but (30, 48) is not in the red pixels. The blue cross center is at... let me figure out where the blue cross center is.
Blue cross: 53 pixels, boundary starts at (29, 48). 
53 = 4 × 13 + 1 (center). So each arm is 13 pixels including center.
The cross extends 12 pixels from center in each direction.
From the boundary: (29, 48) to (42, 61). The vertical arm goes from row 29 to row 42 at col 48. That's 14 pixels. The horizontal arm goes from col 48 to col 61 at some row. That's 14 pixels.
Center is at (35.5, 54.5)? No, let me think again.
Actually, 53 pixels: if the cross is symmetric with arms of equal length, then each arm has (53-1)/4 = 13 pixels. But that includes the center in each arm, so each arm extends 12 pixels from center.
Total: 4 × 13 - 3 (center counted 4 times, subtract 3) = 53 - 3 = 50? No.
Actually: 4 arms × 12 pixels each + 1 center = 49. That's not 53 either.
Hmm, 53 = 4 × 13 - 1 = 51? No, 52. 
Let me just think: if each arm is length L from center (not including center), total = 4L + 1.
4L + 1 = 53, so L = 13. Each arm extends 13 pixels from center.
From the boundary: (29, 48) is the top of the vertical arm. If center is at (29+13, 48) = (42, 48), then the bottom of the vertical arm is at (42+13, 48) = (55, 48). But the boundary shows (42, 48) and (42, 61).
Actually, the boundary is a list of corner points. Let me look at it: [29, 48], [41, 48], [42, 49], [42, 61]. This suggests the structure spans rows 29-42 and cols 48-61.
Vertical arm: rows 29-42 at col 48 (14 pixels)
Horizontal arm: cols 48-61 at row 35.5? That doesn't work.
Let me recalculate: if the center is at row c_r and col c_c:
- Vertical arm: rows [c_r-13, c_r+13] at col c_c
- Horizontal arm: cols [c_c-13, c_c+13] at row c_r
- Total pixels: 27 + 27 - 1 = 53. Yes!
So the vertical arm spans 27 rows and the horizontal arm spans 27 cols.
From the boundary: top at row 29, bottom at row 42. That's 14 rows. But 27 rows would be from c_r-13 to c_r+13.
Hmm, 42 - 29 = 13. So the vertical arm is 14 pixels (rows 29 to 42). That means c_r - 13 = 29, c_r + 13 = 42. So c_r = 42, and c_r - 13 = 29. But 42 - 13 = 29. Yes! And c_r + 13 = 55, not 42. So the vertical arm only goes from row 29 to row 42, which is 14 pixels.
Wait, that means the arm extends 13 pixels UP from center at row 42, but only 0 pixels DOWN. That doesn't make sense for a cross.
Actually, let me reconsider. The boundary is the outer perimeter. For a cross shape, the boundary would trace around the outside. The boundary points [29, 48], [41, 48], [42, 49], [42, 61] suggest:
- Top-left corner of the cross: (29, 48)
- Bottom-left corner: (41, 48)
- Then (42, 49) and (42, 61) are on the right side
This is confusing. Let me just look at the image. The blue cross is in the lower-right area. It has a vertical arm and a horizontal arm.
Looking at the image, the blue cross seems to have its center around row 35-36, col 54-55.
OK, I'm spending too much time analyzing the exact structure. Let me just try SPACE to see what happens and understand the teleportation mechanic.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model updated: Level 2 has 3 structures: orange X (diagonal cross, center ~18,27), red diamond (diagonal, center ~30,39), blue cross (axis-aligned, center ~35-36, 54-55). Player at (18,27) on orange X. 10 gray squares: 3 red-centered, 4 blue-centered, 3 orange-centered. Need to test SPACE to teleport between structures.

action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
